title: [WIP][RFC] skopeo: add support for pulling/seeding images through BitTorrent
body:
Add support for pulling and seeding images through BitTorrent.

Multiple images can be seed at the same time, for each of them it is necessary to specify the remote location and the local storage where they are stored.  It keeps seeding until SIGTERM is received.

```console
$ skopeo copy docker://quay.io/giuseppe/hello-world dir:/tmp/seed
$ skopeo seed --torrent-trackers udp4://192.168.125.222:6969 \
    docker://quay.io/giuseppe/hello-world dir:/tmp/seed

```

For pulling an image using BitTorrent.

```console
$ skopeo copy --torrent-trackers udp4://192.168.125.222:6969 \
   --try-torrent docker://quay.io/giuseppe/hello-world dir:/tmp/test
```

I am using an internal BitTorrent tracker as I am hitting an issue with the quay.io tracker, as I always get " "ERROR 503: Service Unavailable: Back-end server is at capacity" /cc @jzelinskie

comments.body: @jzelinskie do you know who I can ping for help with the quay.io registry and the "ERROR 503: Service Unavailable: Back-end server is at capacity" error I am seeing there?
@giuseppe I'm back from vacation and can take a look. The Quay.io instance of Chihaya has been firing off alarms recently, so I need to investigate our production stack.
@giuseppe got everything fixed with our deployment

@jzelinskie thanks a lot.  It works now.  If anyone is interested to play with it, there is no need to setup a different tracker and it is enough to:

```console
$ skopeo copy --try-torrent docker://quay.io/giuseppe/hello-world dir:/tmp/seed
```

and to seed the layers:
```
$ skopeo seed docker://quay.io/giuseppe/hello-world dir:/tmp/seed
```
How much did the skopeo package grow in size based off this patch?

> How much did the skopeo package grow in size based off this patch?

this has a big impact on the binary size: from ~26Mb to ~38Mb
without debug info the difference is ~16Mb vs ~21Mb
I'm still having problems with the quay tracker, can't start any pull, I'll need to investigate it more but from a quick check I am having the same problem using `quayctl`

@jzelinskie not sure what I am doing wrong but I've tried setting up two DigitalOcean droplets one for seeding and one for pulling using quayctl but the pull always fall back to HTTP, it works if I use a custom tracker.
@giuseppe Needs rebase.  Are you still working on this?  Seems like this would be a nice feature,
I am still blocked with quay.io, last time I tried torrents were not working
@giuseppe I can take a look at this on Monday to unblock you. Do you have steps to reproduce?

> @giuseppe I can take a look at this on Monday to unblock you. Do you have steps to reproduce?

@jzelinskie thanks.  What I am trying to manually do is, fetch the torrent for a layer, e.g.
https://quay.io/c1/torrent/giuseppe/hello-world/blobs/sha256:e71c36a80ba912dd7a5a9f2f2d6136c148afa19bc7d024bd616b74a0bc7a2774

then tried a few different clients for downloading it.  I'm also seeding at the same time from another client, but still the download doesn't start.
@giuseppe There was a misconfiguration in the tracker deployment that made the tracker fail authentication. I tweaked the config and things appear to be working for me now.
thanks, it seems to work now.  I've fixed a couple of issues in the `containers/image` implementation and pushed a new version.

I was talking to a customer about this issue at Kubecon last week, and they were very interested in this feature.  They were looking at this from very large scale.  Basically wanting to seed 1000 machines simultaneously.  Does it become more valuable for that use case?

> I was talking to a customer about this issue at Kubecon last week, and they were very interested in this feature. They were looking at this from very large scale. Basically wanting to seed 1000 machines simultaneously. Does it become more valuable for that use case?

I think so, yes. But it may also depend on the presence of a local cache/mirror. However, I have no experience with an environment of a scale of 1000 nodes pulling simultaneously :^)

@rhatdan Apologies. I saw you out of the corner of my eye at Kubecon, but totally missed a chance to chat with you about this. There really isn't any specification around this work, so a client will pretty much only work with Quay. We've separated every layer into its own individual BitTorrent swarm, which could be different from how others would like to implement it.

That being said, it would be awesome to get this client work merged into Skopeo so that we can deprecate [quayctl](https://github.com/coreos/quayctl) which exists solely to facilitate hackery loading BitTorrent images into the docker CAS.

@jzelinskie Cool, if we can get to the point that Skopeo with particular Registries like Quay work well, we might be able to convince others to support it.   
@giuseppe Needs a rebase.

rebased, I think this must be integrated with blobcache, I'll look at it.

> I was talking to a customer about this issue at Kubecon last week, and they were very interested in this feature. They were looking at this from very large scale. Basically wanting to seed 1000 machines simultaneously. Does it become more valuable for that use case?

I think this is exactly the kind of use case where using Torrents can be useful.  Are these nodes on the public cloud?  Are the images on a private registry?
@giuseppe @vrothberg @mtrmac What is the state of this PR?  Do we want to move forward with it?
